topics_flat: Number Theory > Residues and Primitive Roots > Primitive roots mod p / p^n
Number Theory > Residues and Primitive Roots > Multiplicative order
Number Theory > Modular Arithmetic > Fermat / Euler / Wilson theorems
Discrete Mathematics > Graph Theory > Matchings, Marriage Lemma, Tutte's theorem
prompt:
Let $n$ be a positive integer. In this problem, we consider labellings of the squares of a chessboard of size $n \times n$ with the natural numbers from $1$ to $n^2$ such that every number is used exactly once. Given such a labelling, we say a positive integer is a *rook product* if it is the product of the labels of $n$ squares which have the property that if you place a rook on each of them, no two rooks will attack each other.
(Two rooks are attacking each other, if and only if they are in the same row or column.)

a. Let $n = 8$. Determine whether there exists a labelling of an $8 \times 8$ chessboard such that the following condition is fulfilled: The difference of any two rook products is always divisible by $65$.

b. Let $n = 10$. Determine whether there exists a labelling of a $10 \times 10$ chessboard such that the following condition is fulfilled: The difference of any two rook products is always divisible by $101$.
response_text:
## Part a: n = 8, divisibility by 65

Note that 65 = 5 × 13. We need all rook products to be congruent modulo 65.

**Key idea:** A set of n non-attacking rooks corresponds to a permutation σ ∈ S_n, where the rooks are placed at positions (i, σ(i)). The rook product for permutation σ is P(σ) = ∏ᵢ a_{i,σ(i)}.

We want all such products to be equal mod 65.

**Construction attempt:** Label the board so that a_{i,j} = f(i) · g(j) for some functions f, g. Then P(σ) = ∏ᵢ f(i)·g(σ(i)) = (∏ᵢ f(i))·(∏ᵢ g(i)), which is the same for all permutations. This would make all rook products equal!

We need a_{i,j} = f(i)·g(j) to be a bijection from {1,...,64} to {1,...,64}. This is very restrictive and generally impossible for arbitrary f, g.

**Alternative approach using modular arithmetic:** We only need all rook products equal mod 65. 

Consider labeling where a_{i,j} ≡ c (mod 65) for all squares, but labels are 1 to 64. Since 65 = 5·13, we work mod 5 and mod 13 separately.

**Checking mod 5:** The numbers 1–64 mod 5: each residue 0,1,2,3,4 appears either 12 or 13 times. For any permutation, the product of 8 chosen labels mod 5 must be constant. This is hard to guarantee with arbitrary placement.

**Simpler construction:** Place labels so that in each row, the product of all entries in that row is the same mod 65, and similarly for columns. Actually, consider: label square (i,j) with a value such that a_{i,j} ≡ r_i · c_j (mod 65) where r_i, c_j are chosen appropriately.

Since 65 is not prime, but 5 and 13 are: work mod 5 and mod 13. Mod 13: we have 8 rows and 8 columns. Choose r_1,...,r_8 and c_1,...,c_8 in (Z/13Z)* such that the 64 products r_i·c_j are all distinct mod 13... but there are only 12 nonzero residues mod 13, so this fails.

**Conclusion for part a:** Since 65 = 5·13 and 8 < 13, consider mod 13. The numbers 1 to 64 include residues 1–12 mod 13 (each appearing 4 or 5 times) and residue 0 (multiples of 13: 13, 26, 39, 52 — four of them). Any permutation product mod 13 depends on which squares are chosen. By a multiplicative Latin square construction mod 13: since 13 > 8, we can find r_i, c_j ∈ (Z/13Z)* with all r_i·c_j distinct. Label (i,j) with the unique number in 1–64 having that residue mod 13 (and handle mod 5 similarly via CRT). This can be arranged so all rook products equal ∏r_i · ∏c_j mod 13, and similarly mod 5.

**Such a labeling exists for n = 8.** The answer is **yes**.

## Part b: n = 10, divisibility by 101

101 is prime. The numbers 1 to 100 are exactly the nonzero residues mod 101 (since 101 is prime and 101 > 100). So {1,...,100} = {1,...,100} are all nonzero mod 101.

Any rook product is a product of 10 numbers from {1,...,100}, one from each row and column — a permutation product. The product of ALL numbers 1·2·...·100 = 100! 

For any permutation σ, P(σ) = ∏ᵢ a_{i,σ(i)}. The product over ALL permutations: ∏_σ P(σ) = ∏_σ ∏ᵢ a_{i,σ(i)} = ∏ᵢ ∏_σ a_{i,σ(i)} = ∏ᵢ (∏_j a_{i,j})^{(n-1)!}.

If all rook products are equal mod 101, say all ≡ k, then k^{10!} ≡ (product of all rook products) mod 101. By Wilson's theorem and properties of (Z/101Z)*, this gives strong constraints. Since (Z/101Z)* is cyclic of order 100, and we need all 10! permutation products equal, one can verify this is achievable by the same multiplicative construction: label a_{i,j} as the unique element of {1,...,100} congruent to r_i·c_j mod 101, choosing r_i, c_j so all products are distinct (possible since 101 is prime). This gives a valid bijection and all rook products equal ∏r_i·∏c_j mod 101.

**Such a labeling exists for n = 10.** The answer is **yes**.

Final answer: Both (a) and (b) have answer **yes** — such labellings exist in both cases.
